name: First Recipe: No-Cook Snacks
description: Learners prepare simple no-cook snacks: ants on a log (celery, peanut butter, raisins), fruit kabobs, trail mix, or yogurt parfaits. Focuses on kitchen safety (handwashing, clean surfaces, allergen awareness), measuring and portioning, following simple instructions, and the pride of making your own food. No heat or sharp tools required -- just hands, ingredients, and enthusiasm.
materials: Celery, peanut butter or sunflower butter, raisins
learning_objectives: Practice kitchen hygiene (handwashing, clean surfaces)
Follow simple recipe instructions in order
Measure and portion ingredients
Identify foods by type (fruit, protein, grain)
variations: Allergen-free: substitute sunflower butter for peanut butter, use allergen-safe ingredients
Older learners: write their own no-cook recipe and teach it to younger students
Cultural exchange: each learner brings a no-cook snack from their family tradition

name: Meal Planning for a Week
description: Learners plan all meals for one week within a realistic budget. They research recipes, create a grocery list, compare prices, consider nutrition and variety, and account for time constraints (busy weekday dinners vs. weekend cooking). If possible, coordinate with families to use this as actual meal planning. Integrates cooking, nutrition, budgeting, and time management into one practical exercise.
materials: Grocery store ads or online price lists
learning_objectives: Plan nutritionally varied meals for seven days
Create an organized grocery list from meal plans
Stay within a defined food budget
Account for preparation time and skill level
Incorporate personal and family food preferences
variations: Budget challenge: plan a week of meals for a very limited budget
Cultural focus: plan meals featuring a specific cuisine or food tradition
Family project: interview family members about preferences and plan together
Batch cooking: plan meals that share ingredients to reduce waste and cost

name: Sew a Button Challenge
description: Learners practice sewing a button onto fabric. Starting with threading a needle, they learn the four-hole button sewing technique: secure the thread, pass through holes in a cross or parallel pattern, create a thread shank for thickness, and tie off securely. Timed challenge element adds fun. By the end, every learner has a button they sewed themselves that stays attached when tugged.
learning_objectives: Thread a needle and tie a starting knot
Sew a four-hole button securely using proper technique
Create a thread shank for garment thickness
Tie off thread securely so the button stays attached
Understand when and why to repair rather than replace
variations: Younger learners: use lacing cards or large buttons with blunt needles
Speed challenge: timed competition for secure button attachment
Real repair: bring a garment with a missing button and sew it back on
Two-hole and shank buttons: practice different button types

name: Laundry Sorting & Care Labels
description: Learners practice the complete laundry sorting process: separate by color (whites, lights, darks), by fabric type (delicates vs. sturdy), and by care label instructions. They decode care label symbols (wash temperature, dry method, iron settings, dry clean only) using a reference chart. Then they practice folding techniques for different garment types. Hands-on with actual garments or clean practice items.
materials: Assorted clean garments for sorting practice
learning_objectives: Sort laundry correctly by color and fabric type
Read and interpret care label symbols
Identify garments requiring special handling
Fold different garment types neatly
Understand why sorting matters (color bleeding, fabric damage)
variations: Younger learners: focus on color sorting only (lights and darks)
Stain challenge: add stain identification and treatment selection
Complete cycle: if facilities allow, wash, dry, and fold a real load
Scavenger hunt: find and decode care labels on garments you are wearing

name: Room Deep Clean Challenge
description: Learners deep clean a real room (classroom, common area, or simulated room) from ceiling to floor. They follow a systematic approach: top to bottom, dry before wet, organized before cleaned. Tasks include dusting, wiping surfaces, cleaning glass, vacuuming or sweeping, mopping, and organizing. Includes learning about different cleaning products and their purposes, and green cleaning alternatives.
learning_objectives: Clean a room systematically (top to bottom, dry to wet)
Use appropriate cleaning methods for different surfaces
Understand cleaning product safety and alternatives
Organize a space as part of cleaning
Develop a sense of accomplishment from a clean space
variations: Younger learners: focus on tidying, sweeping, and wiping with water
Green cleaning challenge: clean using only vinegar, baking soda, and water
Timed challenge: team-based cleaning with quality inspection at the end
Maintenance vs. deep clean: discuss the difference and when each is needed

name: Weekly Chore Chart Creator
description: Learners design a weekly chore chart for a household (real or hypothetical). They list all household tasks, estimate time for each, assign frequency (daily, weekly, monthly), and distribute responsibilities fairly among household members. Discusses equity in shared living, age-appropriate contributions, and how systems prevent arguments about who does what.
materials: Large paper or digital design tool
learning_objectives: Identify all regular household cleaning and maintenance tasks
Estimate time needed for different tasks
Create a fair distribution of responsibilities
Design a system that is clear, visible, and trackable
Understand that shared living requires shared responsibility
variations: Personal version: design a chart for your own room or responsibilities
Apartment simulation: plan for a shared living situation with roommates
Digital version: create in a spreadsheet or task management app
Seasonal expansion: add monthly and seasonal tasks to the weekly chart

name: Budget Simulation Game
description: Learners receive a monthly income and must budget for all living expenses: rent, utilities, food, transportation, insurance, phone, clothing, savings, and discretionary spending. Random event cards introduce real-life surprises: car repair, medical bill, job bonus, price increase, unexpected expense. Learners track their budget on a spreadsheet and make decisions about trade-offs. Demonstrates that budgeting is about choices, not deprivation.
learning_objectives: Create a realistic monthly budget with all major expense categories
Make trade-off decisions when income does not cover all wants
Handle unexpected expenses without going into debt
Understand the importance of emergency savings
Recognize that budgeting is a tool for choices, not restriction
variations: Different income levels: simulate budgets at minimum wage, median wage, and higher income
Life stage versions: college student, single adult, family with children
Debt scenario: start with existing debt and work toward paying it off
Savings challenge: compete to build the largest emergency fund over 12 simulated months

name: Grocery Price Comparison
description: Learners compare prices for the same items across different stores, brands, and sizes. They calculate unit price (price per ounce, per count, per serving), compare name brand vs. store brand, and evaluate when buying in bulk is actually cheaper. Uses real grocery ads, online prices, or a simulated store. Develops the habit of comparing value rather than just looking at the sticker price.
materials: Grocery store ads or circulars (2-3 stores)
Sample items with different sizes and brands
learning_objectives: Calculate unit price to compare different sizes and brands
Compare prices across stores for the same items
Evaluate name brand vs. store brand value
Determine when bulk buying saves money and when it does not
Develop the habit of price comparison before purchasing
variations: Younger learners: compare just two items (big vs. small, brand A vs. brand B)
Shopping trip: visit an actual store and practice comparison in person
Online comparison: use grocery delivery websites to compare prices digitally
Meal-based: calculate the total cost of a specific recipe from different stores

name: Fix-It Workshop: Simple Repairs
description: Learners rotate through repair stations practicing common household fixes: tightening a loose screw or hinge, unclogging a drain with a plunger and drain snake, replacing a battery in a device, patching a small hole in drywall, re-gluing a broken object, and changing a light bulb safely. Each station has clear instructions, safety guidance, and the right tools. Emphasis on the confidence that comes from fixing things yourself.
learning_objectives: Identify and safely use basic household tools
Perform common household repairs with confidence
Recognize which repairs are safe to DIY and which need a professional
Develop the habit of assessing repair before replacement
Build confidence in problem-solving with physical tasks
variations: Younger learners: focus on battery replacement, tightening with supervision
Bring broken items from home and attempt repair together
Repair cafe format: set up stations and invite community members to bring items
Cost analysis: calculate money saved by repairing instead of replacing each item

name: Emergency Kit Assembly
description: Learners research, plan, and assemble a basic emergency preparedness kit following Ready.gov guidelines. They discuss what emergencies are common in their area (weather, power outages, natural disasters), what supplies are essential (water, food, flashlight, first aid, important documents), and how to create a family communication plan. Each learner creates a checklist and assembles at least a starter kit. Emphasis on preparedness as responsibility, not fear.
materials: Sample supplies (water bottles, flashlight, batteries, first aid kit)
learning_objectives: Identify common emergencies in their geographic area
List essential emergency supplies and explain why each is needed
Assemble a basic emergency kit
Create a family communication plan for emergencies
Understand preparedness as responsible planning, not paranoia
variations: Younger learners: focus on identifying emergencies and knowing who to call
Car kit version: build an emergency kit for a vehicle
School kit: create a classroom emergency preparedness plan
72-hour challenge: plan supplies for 72 hours without utilities or shopping

name: Container Garden Project
description: Learners plant and begin growing something edible in a container: herbs (basil, mint, parsley), lettuce, cherry tomatoes, or green onions. They learn about soil, sunlight, watering, and the patience required to grow food. Ongoing care responsibility extends beyond the class session. Connects food to its source, develops responsibility, and demonstrates that anyone can grow food -- even in a small space.
materials: Containers (pots, recycled containers with drainage holes)
Seeds or starter plants (herbs, lettuce, tomatoes)
learning_objectives: Plant seeds or starter plants in appropriate containers
Understand basic plant needs (soil, water, light, nutrients)
Develop responsibility for ongoing plant care
Connect food to its growing source
Experience the satisfaction of growing something edible
variations: Windowsill garden: grow herbs on a classroom or home windowsill
Regrow scraps: grow green onions, lettuce, or celery from kitchen scraps (zero cost)
Seed starting: start seeds indoors and transplant when weather allows
Harvest and cook: when plants produce, use the harvest in a cooking activity

name: Repair vs. Replace Debate
description: Learners evaluate real scenarios and debate whether to repair or replace: a phone with a cracked screen, a jacket with a broken zipper, a wobbly chair, a toaster that only toasts on one side, shoes with worn soles. They research repair costs vs. replacement costs, consider environmental impact, evaluate time investment, and make a reasoned decision. Develops critical thinking about consumer choices and the throwaway culture.
materials: Scenario cards with broken item descriptions
Repair cost research resources (iFixit, local repair shops)
Decision matrix worksheet (cost, time, environmental impact, quality)
learning_objectives: Evaluate whether repairing or replacing an item is the better choice
Research and compare repair costs vs. replacement costs
Consider environmental impact in consumer decisions
Factor in time, skill level, and quality in repair decisions
Develop a thoughtful approach to consumer choices
variations: Environmental focus: calculate landfill impact of discarding vs. repairing
Bring real items: evaluate actual broken items from home
Interview a repair professional: learn what repairs are worthwhile
Create a decision flowchart: build a visual tool for repair vs. replace decisions
